title: Cutting a rabbet by hand
subtitle: Marking and chiselling a clean rebate without a power router
excerpt: A rabbet (rebate) is a step cut along the edge or face of a piece of wood; the joint that holds the back of a picture frame, the shoulder of a shadow box, or the housing for a glass panel. This technique covers marking, sawing the shoulder, and chiselling the floor using only hand tools from a basic kit.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for hand-cut rebate method; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for chisel rabbet technique in seasoned hardwood.

content.text: A
rabbet

is one of the most common joints in woodwork; a step cut into the edge of a piece so another piece can sit flush inside it. Routing is the modern method; hand-chiselling predates it by centuries and produces a result indistinguishable in quality when done correctly. The method uses a marking knife for the shoulder boundary, a saw for the shoulder cut, and chisels for the floor.
Seasoned wood
of any species is workable with this technique; the method varies slightly by grain direction.
Defining the rabbet

A rabbet has two dimensions: width (how far in from the edge) and depth (how far down from the face). For a shadow box back panel in 3 mm hardboard, the rabbet is 6 mm wide and 4 mm deep; the hardboard sits flush with the face when inserted. For a glass panel in a picture frame, the rabbet is 8 mm wide and 6 mm deep. Mark both dimensions in pencil before picking up a tool, and double-check the mating piece before cutting; a rabbet that is 1 mm too narrow for the panel it is intended to house cannot be corrected by enlarging it while keeping the shoulder clean.
Step-by-step

Mark the shoulder line (the inner edge of the rabbet, parallel to the workpiece edge) with a marking knife and a square. Score once, then re-score in the same line with firm pressure; the scored line must be 0.5 mm deep to guide the saw reliably. Run the knife line across both faces and around the end grain if the rabbet runs the full length of the piece.
Mark the depth line on the edge of the workpiece with a marking gauge set to the rabbet depth. Run the gauge from the face to mark the depth line along the full edge within the rabbet area.
Saw the shoulder cut: position the saw on the waste side of the scored line so the
kerf
falls entirely in the waste, not in the finished shoulder face. Saw down to the depth line on the edge face; no further. Check from both ends of the cut that the saw reached the depth line equally.

Remove the waste with the 25 mm firmer chisel. Work with the grain from the edge inward, chisel bevel down, removing 2 to 3 mm of depth per pass. Make a horizontal split toward the saw cut at the depth line; the waste piece lifts free. Repeat until the floor reaches the depth line across the full width.

Flatten the floor with the 12 mm bevel-edge chisel held flat, bevel up, using hand pressure alone. Work parallel to the grain in light paring strokes. Test with a straightedge; the floor must be flat across its full width or the mating piece will rock.

Trim the shoulder face: set the 12 mm chisel vertically in the scored shoulder line and pare the wall back to the line with light mallet taps. The shoulder face must be exactly at 90 degrees to the floor; check with a small square.
Testing the fit

Slide the panel into the rabbet. It should drop in with light hand pressure and sit flush with the face. If the panel rocks, the floor is not flat; find the high point by pressing the panel and looking for where it pivots. If the panel sits below flush, the rabbet is too deep; this cannot be corrected without rebuilding. Always cut the rabbet 0.5 mm shallower than the target and deepen in passes.
Common mistakes
Saw kerf in the finished shoulder face: the shoulder will show a step or a gap when the mating panel is inserted. Score the line deeper and saw on the waste side only.

Chiselling against the grain when removing waste: the grain lifts ahead of the chisel and tears below the intended depth. Work with the grain direction; in most straight-grained wood this is from one end toward the other.
Depth inconsistent across the rabbet width: the floor slopes and the panel rocks. Check depth with a small ruler at 4 to 5 points across the width before paring the final surface.
